en.extract:
The western or Arfak parotia (Parotia sefilata) is a medium-sized, approximately 33 cm long, bird-of-paradise with a medium-length tail.
Parotia comes from the Greek parotis, a lock or curl of hair by the ear, alluding to the head wires. The specific name sefilata is derived from the Latin word 'sex', meaning six, and filum, a thread or filament.


Description
Like other birds-of-paradise, the western parotia is sexually dimorphic. The male has jet black plumage, with striking iridescent scale-like golden-green breast shields and triangular silver feathers on its crown. The occipital plumes (or head wires) arise from above and behind the eyes, with three long erectile wire-like plumes with smaller spatulate tips, above and behind each eye. As with most members of the family, the female is unadorned and has brown plumage. The species is similar to Lawes's parotia (Parotia lawesii).


Distribution
The western parotia is found only in the mountain forests of Vogelkop and the Wandammen Peninsula of Western New Guinea.


Behaviour

The species is polygynous. Males presumably perform a series of courtship displays likely on terrestrial courts in exploded leks. 
In courtship display, the male performs a ballerina-like dance with its elongated black plumes spread skirt-like, right below the iridescent breast shield. During the spectacular dance, he shakes his head and neck rapidly to show the brilliance of his inverted silver triangle-shaped head adornment to attending females. 
The nest is built and attended by the female alone; the breeding season is largely unknown.
The diet consists mainly of fruits such as figs, and arthropods.


Status
A widespread and common species throughout its range, the western parotia is evaluated as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. It is listed in Appendix II of CITES.


References


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet
Audio and Video from the Macaulay Library

The '''western''' or '''Arfak parotia''' ('''''Parotia sefilata''''') is a medium-sized, approximately 33 cm long, [[bird-of-paradise]] with a medium-length tail.

Parotia comes from the [[Greek language|Greek]] ''parotis'', a lock or curl of hair by the ear, alluding to the head wires. The specific name ''sefilata'' is derived from the [[Latin]] word 'sex', meaning six, and ''filum'', a thread or filament.<ref name="gregory">{{cite book |last1=Gregory |first1=Phil |title=Birds of paradise and bowerbirds |date=2020 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |location=London |isbn=978-1-4729-7584-3}}</ref>

==Description==
Like other birds-of-paradise, the western parotia is [[sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]]. The male has jet black plumage, with striking [[iridescence|iridescent]] scale-like golden-green breast shields and triangular silver feathers on its crown.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Mobley |first1=Jason A. |title=Birds of the World |year=2008 |publisher=Marshall Cavendish |isbn=978-0-7614-7775-4 |language=en}}</ref> The occipital plumes (or head wires) arise from above and behind the eyes, with three long [[erectile]] wire-like plumes with smaller [[spatula]]te tips, above and behind each eye.<ref name="gregory"/> As with most members of the [[Paradisaeidae|family]], the female is unadorned and has brown plumage.<ref name="AusMuseum"> {{cite web |last=Hugill |first=Michael |year=2011 |title=Western Parotia |url=http://australianmuseum.net.au/Western-Parotia |website=The Australian Museum |language=en}}</ref> The species is similar to [[Lawes's parotia]] (''Parotia lawesii'').

==Distribution==
The western parotia is found only in the mountain forests of [[Vogelkop]] and the [[Wandammen Peninsula]] of [[Western New Guinea]].<ref name="AusMuseum"/>

==Behaviour==
[[File:Western Parotia Court 1138.jpg|thumb|Western Parotia at his court]]
The species is [[Polygyny in animals|polygynous]]. Males presumably perform a series of courtship displays likely on terrestrial courts in [[Lek_mating#Lekking Behaviour|exploded leks]].<ref name="AusMuseum"/> 

In courtship display, the male performs a [[ballerina]]-like dance with its elongated black plumes spread [[skirt]]-like, right below the iridescent breast shield. During the spectacular dance, he shakes his head and neck rapidly to show the brilliance of his inverted silver [[triangle]]-shaped head adornment to attending females. 

The nest is built and attended by the female alone; the breeding season is largely unknown.<ref name="gregory"/>

The diet consists mainly of fruits such as figs, and arthropods.<ref name="AusMuseum"/>

==Status==
A widespread and common species throughout its range, the western parotia is evaluated as Least Concern on the [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> It is listed in Appendix II of [[CITES]].
